a.question: How many players are on a cricket team on the field?
a.explanation: Each cricket side fields eleven players.

a.question: What do we call the wooden sticks a batter defends in cricket?
a.explanation: Three stumps stand behind each batter.

a.question: What is it called when a bowler gets a batter out in cricket?
a.explanation: Taking a wicket means dismissing a batter.

a.question: Which country is known as the birthplace of cricket?
a.explanation: Cricket began in England centuries ago.

a.question: What is the main way to score in cricket, by running between the wickets?
a.explanation: Batters run to score runs.

a.question: Who hits the ball in cricket, trying to score runs?
a.explanation: The batter uses a bat to hit the ball.

a.question: What do we call the person who makes decisions during a cricket match?
a.explanation: Umpires make the on-field calls in cricket.

a.question: What do we call it when a batter hits the ball over the boundary without it touching the ground?
a.explanation: A six is worth six runs.

a.question: What is a 'century' in cricket?
a.explanation: A century is a personal score of 100 runs or more.

a.question: Which famous trophy is contested between England and Australia in cricket?
a.explanation: The Ashes has been played since 1882.

a.question: Which country won the very first Cricket World Cup in 1975?
a.explanation: The West Indies won the inaugural tournament.

a.question: How many balls are usually bowled in one over in cricket?
a.explanation: A standard over has six balls.

a.question: How many players are on a football (soccer) team on the field?
a.explanation: Each football team fields eleven players.

a.question: What is the object players kick to score in football?
a.explanation: Football is played with a round ball.

a.question: What do we call scoring in football?
a.explanation: Scoring in football is called a goal.

a.question: Which tournament is the biggest football competition between national teams, held every four years?
a.explanation: The FIFA World Cup is football's biggest prize.

a.question: How often is the FIFA World Cup held?
a.explanation: It takes place once every four years.

a.question: Which country hosted the very first FIFA World Cup in 1930?
a.explanation: Uruguay hosted and won the first World Cup.

a.question: Which continent's national teams compete in the Copa America?
a.explanation: Copa America is South America's championship.

a.question: Which football tournament features European national teams like England, France, and Germany?
a.explanation: It is nicknamed the Euros.

a.question: What do we call the player who stops the ball from going into the goal?
a.explanation: The goalkeeper guards the goal.

a.question: What colour card does a referee show for a serious foul, sending a player off?
a.explanation: A red card means the player must leave the match.

a.question: Which country has won the most FIFA World Cups?
a.explanation: Brazil has won it five times.

a.question: How long is a standard football match, not counting extra time?
a.explanation: A match is two 45-minute halves.

a.question: What does F1 stand for?
a.explanation: F1 is short for Formula One racing.

a.question: What is an F1 car built for?
a.explanation: F1 cars are the fastest racing cars on tracks.

a.question: What do we call a quick stop during a race to change tyres?
a.explanation: Cars dash into the pits for fresh tyres.

a.question: What does the chequered flag signal in a race?
a.explanation: The chequered flag waves as the race ends.

a.question: What is the closed road or track F1 cars race on called?
a.explanation: F1 races happen on circuits.

a.question: What is awarded to the F1 driver with the most points at the end of the season?
a.explanation: The top scorer becomes World Champion.

a.question: What do we call starting an F1 race from the very front, after setting the fastest qualifying time?
a.explanation: Pole position is the best starting spot.

a.question: Roughly how fast can an F1 car go at top speed?
a.explanation: F1 cars can reach speeds near 350 km/h.

a.question: What safety gear must every F1 driver wear on their head?
a.explanation: Helmets protect drivers at high speed.

a.question: What is the name for the team members who service the car during a pit stop?
a.explanation: The pit crew changes tyres in seconds.

a.question: How many wheels does a Formula 1 car have?
a.explanation: Like most cars, an F1 car has four wheels.

a.question: What colour flag warns drivers of danger on the track?
a.explanation: A yellow flag means slow down, danger ahead.

a.question: What is the name of the famous street race in F1 held in Monaco?
a.explanation: It winds through the streets of Monte Carlo.

a.question: What do we call a single race event in the F1 calendar?
a.explanation: Each F1 race weekend is called a Grand Prix.

a.question: What is the goalkeeper's main job in football?
options: Stopping the other team from scoring
a.explanation: Goalkeepers defend their team's goal.

a.question: In cricket, what is the ball made of?
a.explanation: A cricket ball has a cork core wrapped in leather.

a.question: Who is this footballer, an Argentine star who has won multiple Ballon d'Or awards?
a.explanation: Messi has won a record number of Ballon d'Or awards.

a.question: Who is this footballer, a Portuguese star famous for his powerful jump and goal-scoring record?
a.explanation: Ronaldo is one of football's all-time top scorers.

a.question: Who is this cricketer, a former Indian captain known for his calm 'Captain Cool' nickname?
a.explanation: Dhoni led India to World Cup and Champions Trophy wins.

a.question: Who is this cricketer, a modern Indian batting star known for his intensity on the field?
a.explanation: Kohli is one of the most prolific run-scorers of his generation.

b.question: What are the three main formats of international cricket?
b.explanation: Cricket is played in three main formats today.

b.question: How many days can a Test cricket match last?
b.explanation: Test matches are scheduled over five days.

b.question: What is a 'duck' in cricket?
options: A batter out for zero runs
b.explanation: Being out for zero is called a duck.

b.question: What does 'LBW' stand for in cricket?
b.explanation: LBW is a way a batter can be given out.

b.question: What is a 'hat-trick' in cricket?
options: A bowler taking three wickets with three consecutive balls
b.explanation: It's a rare and celebrated bowling feat.

b.question: Which format of cricket is the shortest, lasting about 3 hours?
b.explanation: T20 cricket is the fast, short format.

b.question: What does IPL stand for in cricket?
b.explanation: The IPL is a major T20 league in India.

b.question: Who is this cricket legend, holder of the record for most international centuries?
b.explanation: Tendulkar scored 100 international centuries.

b.question: What is a 'googly' in cricket bowling?
options: A spin delivery that turns unexpectedly
b.explanation: It deceives batters expecting the ball to turn the other way.

b.question: What is the maximum number of overs per side in a One Day International (ODI)?
b.explanation: ODIs are played over 50 overs per side.

b.question: Which country won the 2022 FIFA World Cup?
b.explanation: Argentina won on penalties in Qatar.

b.question: Which competition is often called the oldest international football tournament, held among South American nations?
b.explanation: Copa America dates back to 1916.

b.question: Which European tournament is held every four years featuring the continent's national teams?
b.explanation: It's commonly known as the Euros.

b.question: Which country won UEFA Euro 2020 (played in 2021)?
b.explanation: Italy beat England on penalties in the final.

b.question: What is offside in football?
options: A player gaining an illegal positional advantage when the ball is played to them
Kicking the ball out of bounds
b.explanation: It stops attackers camping near the goal.

b.question: How many FIFA World Cups has Brazil won?
b.explanation: Brazil has won it a record five times.

b.question: What is a 'hat-trick' in football?
options: Three goals by one player in a match
b.explanation: Scoring three goals earns a hat-trick.

b.question: Which competition is the top European club football tournament?
b.explanation: Europe's top clubs compete for this trophy.

b.question: What does VAR stand for in football?
b.explanation: VAR reviews key decisions using video replays.

b.question: Which countries co-hosted the 2026 FIFA World Cup alongside the USA?
b.explanation: It's the first World Cup hosted by three nations.

b.question: Who is this football legend, nicknamed 'The King' after winning three World Cups with Brazil?
b.explanation: Pele won in 1958, 1962, and 1970.

b.question: What does 'DRS' stand for in Formula 1?
b.explanation: It opens a rear wing flap to help overtaking.

b.question: Roughly how many races make up a modern F1 season?
b.explanation: Modern seasons run over twenty-plus races.

b.question: What is the term for the starting formation of cars before an F1 race begins?
b.explanation: Cars line up on the starting grid.

b.question: Which two drivers are tied for the most F1 World Championships, with 7 each?
b.explanation: Both have won seven drivers' titles.

b.question: What does 'podium' mean in F1?
options: The top three finishers of a race
b.explanation: The top three celebrate on the podium.

b.question: What is 'downforce' in F1 car design?
options: Aerodynamic force pushing the car down for better grip
b.explanation: Wings and the floor generate downforce.

b.question: What is a 'safety car' used for in F1?
options: Slowing the race down during hazards on track
b.explanation: It neutralizes racing when there's danger on track.

b.question: How many teams typically compete in a modern F1 season?
b.explanation: Ten teams (twenty cars) is the usual grid size.

b.question: What does qualifying determine in F1?
options: The starting grid order for the race
b.explanation: Faster laps in qualifying earn a better grid spot.

b.question: Which historic circuit in the United Kingdom is one of the oldest still on the F1 calendar?
b.explanation: Silverstone hosted the first World Championship race in 1950.

b.question: What is a 'Grand Prix' in motorsport?
options: A single race event in the F1 calendar
b.explanation: Each F1 race weekend is called a Grand Prix.

b.question: What signals the end of an F1 race?
b.explanation: It waves as the leader crosses the finish line.

b.question: Which team has won the most F1 Constructors' Championships?
b.explanation: Ferrari has the most Constructors' titles in F1 history.

b.question: Who is this driver, a seven-time F1 World Champion from Germany who dominated the 2000s?
b.explanation: Schumacher won five straight titles with Ferrari.

b.question: Who is this driver, a British seven-time F1 World Champion known for his time at Mercedes?
b.explanation: Hamilton is tied for the most drivers' titles in F1 history.

b.question: Who is this cricketer, an Australian spin bowling legend regarded as one of the greatest bowlers ever?
b.explanation: Warne took over 700 Test wickets with his leg-spin.

b.question: Who is this footballer, a Brazilian forward known for his flair and famous for playing at Barcelona and PSG?
b.explanation: Neymar became one of the most expensive transfers ever.

c.question: What is the Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method used for in cricket?
options: Recalculating targets in rain-affected limited-overs matches
c.explanation: It fairly adjusts targets when overs are lost.

c.question: What is 'reverse swing' in fast bowling?
options: The ball moving opposite to its expected swing direction, usually late in an innings
c.explanation: Ball wear and shine cause this late-innings effect.

c.question: Which cricket formats introduced the 'Super Over' to break ties?
c.explanation: A Super Over decides ties in limited-overs cricket.

c.question: What is a 'yorker' in cricket bowling?
options: A delivery aimed at the batter's feet or the base of the stumps
c.explanation: Yorkers are hard to hit cleanly, aimed at the blockhole.

c.question: Which country's cricket team is nicknamed the 'Baggy Greens'?
c.explanation: Australian Test players wear the famous baggy green cap.

c.question: What does 'declaring an innings' mean in Test cricket?
options: The batting captain voluntarily ends the innings before all batters are out
c.explanation: Captains declare to leave enough time to bowl the opposition out.

c.question: What is the 'follow-on' rule in Test cricket?
options: Forcing the team that batted second to bat again immediately if far enough behind
c.explanation: It's enforced when the deficit passes a set threshold.

c.question: Who is this batter, holder of the highest Test batting average in history at 99.94?
c.explanation: Bradman's average remains cricket's most famous statistic.

c.question: What is a 'Mankad' dismissal in cricket?
options: Running out the non-striker for backing up too far before the ball is bowled
A no-ball called by the umpire
c.explanation: Named after Indian bowler Vinoo Mankad.

c.question: What does the pre-match coin toss decide in cricket?
options: Which captain chooses to bat or field first
c.explanation: Winning the toss can be a tactical advantage.

c.question: Which cricket format is played over up to five days and four innings?
c.explanation: Test cricket is the longest and oldest format.

c.question: What is the significance of scoring a 'century of centuries', a feat achieved by Sachin Tendulkar?
options: Reaching 100 international centuries across a career
c.explanation: No other player has reached 100 international centuries.

c.question: What is the ICC Champions Trophy?
options: A major limited-overs tournament for top cricket nations
c.explanation: It's one of cricket's biggest ODI tournaments.

c.question: Which country won the very first UEFA European Championship in 1960?
c.explanation: The Soviet Union won the inaugural tournament.

c.question: What tactical formation uses four defenders, four midfielders, and two forwards?
c.explanation: 4-4-2 was a dominant formation for decades.

c.question: What is an 'offside trap' used by football defenses?
options: Defenders stepping up together to catch attackers offside
c.explanation: It requires precise defensive coordination.

c.question: Which competition is Europe's secondary club football tournament, alongside the Champions League?
c.explanation: It offers a second European route for clubs.

c.question: Which South American club competition is the equivalent of Europe's Champions League?
c.explanation: It's South America's premier club tournament.

c.question: What is 'Total Football,' pioneered by the Dutch national team in the 1970s?
options: A fluid tactical system where outfield players interchange positions
A rule about total goals scored
c.explanation: Any outfield player could take over any position.

c.question: Who is this player, scorer of the controversial 'Hand of God' goal in the 1986 World Cup?
c.explanation: Maradona punched the ball into the net against England.

c.question: What is a 'false nine' in football tactics?
options: A forward who drops deep instead of staying as a traditional striker
c.explanation: It confuses defenders used to marking a fixed striker.

c.question: What is the significance of the 1950 World Cup's 'Maracanazo'?
options: Uruguay's shocking win over host Brazil in the deciding match
The first televised World Cup final
c.explanation: It remains one of football's biggest upsets.

c.question: How many teams are set to qualify for the FIFA World Cup finals under the expanded 2026 format?
c.explanation: The tournament expanded from 32 to 48 teams.

c.question: What is 'ground effect' aerodynamics in modern F1 car design?
options: Using the car's floor shape to create suction-like downforce
c.explanation: It returned to F1 cars in the 2022 regulations.

c.question: What was significant about the 1.6-litre V6 turbo-hybrid engines introduced in F1 in 2014?
options: They combined a turbocharged engine with energy recovery systems for efficiency
They replaced V8 engines with V12s
c.explanation: It made F1 engines dramatically more fuel-efficient.

c.question: What is 'parc ferme' in F1 regulations?
options: A period where car setup changes are restricted after qualifying
c.explanation: It keeps teams from making major changes before the race.

c.question: Who is this driver, F1's youngest World Champion in 2010 at just 23 years old?
c.explanation: Vettel held the youngest-champion record from 2010 for over a decade.

c.question: What is 'tyre degradation' in F1 race strategy?
options: Tyres losing grip over a race distance, shaping pit stop strategy
A penalty for qualifying too fast
c.explanation: Teams plan pit stops around how fast tyres wear.

c.question: What is the 'halo' device introduced in F1 in 2018?
options: A cockpit protection structure shielding the driver's head
A new type of steering wheel
c.explanation: It has prevented several serious head injuries since introduction.

c.question: Which F1 team, founded in 1929, is the oldest still competing today?
c.explanation: Ferrari is the only team to have competed in every F1 season.

c.question: What does the 'DRS zone' restriction control in F1?
options: Where a rear wing flap may open to aid overtaking
How much fuel a car can carry
How long a pit stop can last
c.explanation: DRS can only be used in specific track zones.

c.question: What does the FIA regulate in Formula 1?
options: The technical and sporting rules of the championship
c.explanation: The FIA is motorsport's governing body.

c.question: What is the 'cost cap' introduced in modern F1 regulations?
options: A limit on how much teams can spend developing their cars each season
A limit on driver salaries only
c.explanation: It aims to level competition between rich and poor teams.

c.question: What is 'graining' in F1 tyre performance?
options: A type of tyre wear causing loss of grip from small rubber tears
c.explanation: Graining can temporarily hurt a car's grip mid-stint.

c.question: Who is this driver, a Brazilian F1 legend whose fierce 1980s rivalry with Alain Prost at McLaren is legendary?
c.explanation: Senna won three World Championships before his death in 1994.

c.question: What is 'slipstreaming' (or drafting) in motor racing?
options: Following closely behind another car to reduce air resistance and gain speed
A type of pit stop procedure
c.explanation: The trailing car experiences less drag in the leader's wake.

c.question: What is the key difference between F1's Constructors' and Drivers' Championships?
options: One rewards a team's combined points, the other an individual driver's points
They are exactly the same award
The Constructors' title only counts wins
The Drivers' title only counts pole positions
c.explanation: Both titles are awarded each season, based on different scoring.

c.question: Who is this cricketer, a West Indian all-rounder often ranked among the greatest cricketers ever?
c.explanation: Sobers was famed for his batting, bowling, and fielding all-round skill.

c.question: Who is this driver, a four-time F1 World Champion for Red Bull who dominated the early 2020s?
c.explanation: Verstappen won consecutive titles from 2021 onward.

c.question: Who is this footballer, a French forward who won the World Cup as a teenager in 2018?
c.explanation: Mbappe became a World Cup winner at just 19 years old.
